package.heading: Typical circuits for 3SK1 Standard solid-state basic units or 3SK1 Advanced basic units
sheets: Table 7-8 (Typical circuit 8): EMERGENCY STOP two-channel, with cross-circuit detection, with monitored start
Table 7-9 (Typical circuit 9): Two-channel, with cross-circuit detection, with autostart
package.note: This is a manufacturer manual/application page showing two typical application circuits, not a project cabinet wiring set. No project drawing number, cabinet ID, or wire numbers are printed.

devices.detail: Named applicable device: '3SK1112 Standard solid-state and 3SK112/3SK122 Advanced'. Two-channel monitoring unit with DIP switches 1-4 and SET/RESET.
Advanced basic units listed as alternative applicable devices for the same circuit.
Output contactor switching the motor load; shown breaking three phases to motor M. Also provides a feedback contact into the monitored-start loop (circuit 8).

cross_sheet_notes: Two tables on one page show the SAME basic unit and terminal set (A1, INK, T1, IN1, T2, IN2, INF, INS, A2) wired to the same Q1/Qn contactors and motor M. They differ ONLY in DIP-switch 1: Table 7-8 = ON/right = Monitored Start (with a Q1 feedback + start button); Table 7-9 = OFF/left = Autostart. In both, DIP 2 = ON (cross-fault detection ON) and DIP 3 = ON (1 double-channel sensor). Both circuits achieve PL e (ISO 13849-1) and SILCL 3 (IEC 62061).

brief.sheet_title: Manual page 128 — two typical application circuits for a Siemens 3SK1 two-channel safety relay driving contactors Q1/Qn and a motor M (Table 7-8 E-STOP with monitored start; Table 7-9 with autostart).
brief.purpose: These are wiring examples for a 3SK1 safety relay (types 3SK1112 Standard solid-state, or 3SK112/3SK122 Advanced). A two-channel safety sensor with two normally-closed contacts (e.g. an EMERGENCY STOP in Table 7-8) feeds inputs IN1 and IN2. The unit sends test pulses out of T1/T2 and reads them back on IN1/IN2 so a short between the two channels (cross-circuit / cross-fault) is detected — this is enabled by DIP switch 2 = ON. When both channels are healthy and closed, the relay energizes and its enable output picks up two contactors, Q1 and Qn, which each switch three phases to motor M; using two contactors gives the redundancy needed for PL e / SILCL 3. The only difference between the two circuits is the start behaviour: in Table 7-8 DIP 1 is ON = 'Monitored Start' (operator must press a start button and the relay checks the Q1 feedback contact before enabling), while in Table 7-9 DIP 1 is OFF = 'Autostart' (the output enables automatically once inputs are healthy). Supply is applied to A1 (+) / A2 (-) across the L+ / L- rails.

brief.troubleshooting_example: Symptom: the safety unit will not enable and the motor won't start. Because cross-circuit detection is ON (DIP 2) and it is a two-channel NC sensor, check BOTH channels discriminatingly. First, with the circuit de-energized and locked out, verify continuity through each sensor contact into IN1 and into IN2 with the E-STOP released: an open contact or broken wire on one channel keeps the unit tripped. If both input channels read closed but the unit still won't set, the fault is instead in the start path — for Table 7-8 (Monitored Start) confirm the start button operates and the Q1 feedback contact into the start loop (INF/INS) is closed when Q1 is dropped out; for Table 7-9 (Autostart) there is no start button, so a no-enable with good inputs points to the unit's output stage or the contactor coils Q1/Qn themselves. Distinguishing points: if IN1/IN2 are both good but the enable/feedback loop at the start terminals is open, it is the start wiring/feedback, not the sensor. NOTE: the internal enable/reset logic lives inside the 3SK1 unit and is NOT drawn on this page, so its exact response cannot be read from this drawing.
brief.safety_context: For any continuity/resistance checks of the sensor contacts, input channels, or feedback loop, de-energize, lock out, and verify absence of voltage first. The control supply is shown as L+ / L- (DC), but NO voltage value is printed on this page — do not assume a level; obtain it from the device rating plate or datasheet. The motor branch (Q1/Qn to M) carries three-phase power; treat it as live and separate from the control-supply testing. This is a generic manual example, so it does not prove the actual field wiring or present energization state of any specific installation.
